title: Controller Mode Change Detection
app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) ┬╖ `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905)
spl:
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id IN ("PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-MODE*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-RUN*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-STOP*")
| table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
| sort -_time
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
description: OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes тАФ each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.
value: OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes тАФ each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.
implementation: Both platforms detect controller mode changes across industrial protocols. Alert immediately тАФ CPU Stop and Offline events are highest priority as they halt physical processes. Cross-reference with scheduled maintenance. Track frequency of mode changes per controller. Investigate any mode change from unexpected source IPs.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
тАв Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) ┬╖ `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905).
тАв Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` ┬╖ Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING").
тАв For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 тАФ Configure data collection
Both platforms detect controller mode changes across industrial protocols. Alert immediately тАФ CPU Stop and Offline events are highest priority as they halt physical processes. Cross-reference with scheduled maintenance. Track frequency of mode changes per controller. Investigate any mode change from unexpected source IPs.

Step 2 тАФ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id IN ("PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-MODE*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-RUN*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-STOP*")
| table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
| sort -_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**Controller Mode Change Detection** тАФ OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes тАФ each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` ┬╖ Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING"). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) ┬╖ `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedтАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nozomi; **sourcetype**: nozomi:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

тАв Scopes the data: index=nozomi, sourcetype="nozomi:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
тАв Pipeline stage (see **Controller Mode Change Detection**): table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
тАв Orders rows with `sort` тАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Controller Mode Change Detection** тАФ OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes тАФ each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` ┬╖ Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING"). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) ┬╖ `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedтАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

тАв Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` тАФ enable acceleration for that model.
тАв Orders rows with `sort` тАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 тАФ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 тАФ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Mode change timeline with color-coded severity; controller status dashboard; unauthorized source detection.
visualization: Mode change timeline with color-coded severity; controller status dashboard; unauthorized source detection.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern тАФ correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
